verses.commentary: Truly I tell you, some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God come with power. Prophetic promise. Fulfilled in the Transfiguration (six days later), the resurrection, or Pentecost. The kingdom is breaking in now, not just future.
After six days, Jesus takes Peter, James, John up a high mountain. Inner circle. Alone. He was transfigured (metamorphoō, μεταμορφόω). Transformed. His true glory breaks through.
His clothes dazzling white, whiter than any earthly bleaching. Radiant glory. Divine light. The veil of flesh momentarily pulled back.

Elijah and Moses appear, talking with Jesus. Law and Prophets converge on Jesus. Moses gave the law; Elijah represents the prophets. Both point to him. Luke tells us they speak of his exodus he will accomplish in Jerusalem (Luke 9:31).

Peter: Rabbi, it is good to be here. Let us make three shelters. Impulse to preserve the moment. He equates Moses and Elijah with Jesus. Misunderstanding. He wants to stay on the mountain, avoid the cross.
Mark explains: He did not know what to say; they were so frightened. Terrified babbling. Human response to divine glory.

A cloud covers them. God's presence (Shekinah). Voice from the cloud: This is my Son, whom I love. Listen to him! Echo of the baptism (1:11), now with added command. Listen to him, not Moses or Elijah. Jesus supersedes all. The transfigured Messiah is the authoritative Word.
Suddenly they see no one except Jesus. Moses and Elijah vanish. Only Jesus remains. He is the fulfillment.
Coming down, Jesus orders them: Tell no one until the Son of Man has risen from the dead. Messianic secret again. Resurrection is the key. Only after the cross and empty tomb will this make sense.
They kept silent but discussed among themselves what rising from the dead meant. Confusion. Resurrection doctrine existed (Daniel 12:2), but they cannot conceive Jesus dying.
Why do teachers say Elijah must come first? Malachi 4:5–6. If Jesus is Messiah, where is Elijah?
Jesus: Elijah does come first and restores all things. But why is it written that the Son of Man must suffer and be rejected? He links Elijah's coming with his own suffering. Both prophesied.
Elijah has come, and they did to him whatever they wished. John the Baptist is Elijah (1:6). He was beheaded (6:27). The forerunner's fate prefigures the Messiah's.
They return to the other disciples. Large crowd, teachers of the law arguing with them. Conflict while Jesus was on the mountain. Glory above, chaos below.
People see Jesus, are overwhelmed with wonder, run to him. His appearance still carries traces of glory? Or simply surprise at his arrival?
What are you arguing about? Jesus enters the fray.
A man: I brought my son, possessed by a spirit that makes him mute. Your disciples could not drive it out. Failed exorcism. Public embarrassment for the nine disciples.
The spirit seizes him, throws him down, foaming, gnashing, rigid. Violent, destructive demon. The disciples, given authority (6:7), now powerless.
You unbelieving generation, how long shall I stay with you? How long shall I put up with you? Exasperation. Universal rebuke: disciples, crowd, scribes. Unbelief pervades all. Bring the boy to me.
The spirit sees Jesus, immediately convulses the boy. Demonic response to divine presence. The boy falls, rolls, foams. Intensified attack.
Jesus asks: How long has he been like this? From childhood. Long-standing bondage.
It has often thrown him into fire or water to kill him. Suicidal demon. But if you can do anything, take pity and help us. Desperate plea, but weak faith. If you can.
If you can? Everything is possible for one who believes. Jesus challenges the if. The issue is not his ability but the man's faith.
Immediately: I do believe; help me overcome my unbelief! Honest confession. Faith and doubt coexist. He asks for help with his faith itself. Perfect prayer.
Crowd running to the scene. Jesus rebukes the spirit: You deaf and mute spirit, come out and never enter him again. Authority. Permanent expulsion commanded.
The spirit shrieks, convulses him violently, comes out. Boy looks dead. Many say: He's dead. Final, destructive act. Demons leave violently.
Jesus takes him by the hand, lifts him up, he stands. Resurrection imagery. From death to life. Jesus raises what demons destroy.
Indoors, disciples ask privately: Why couldn't we drive it out? Honest question. They were given authority (6:7, 13), used it successfully, but now failed.
This kind comes out only by prayer. Dependence, not technique. Some demons require deeper spiritual engagement. Power flows from intimacy with God.
They pass through Galilee. Jesus does not want anyone to know. Seeking privacy for discipleship training.
He teaches: The Son of Man will be delivered into the hands of men. They will kill him. After three days he will rise. Second passion prediction. More specific: betrayal, execution, resurrection.
They did not understand and were afraid to ask. Persistent incomprehension. Fear of clarification. They sense something ominous but avoid it.
In Capernaum, Jesus asks: What were you arguing about on the road? He knows already.
They kept quiet because they argued about who was the greatest. Shame. Jesus predicts his death; they debate their rank. Grotesque contrast.
Jesus sits, calls the Twelve. Formal teaching posture. Anyone who wants to be first must be last and servant of all. Kingdom inversion. Greatness redefined. Service, not status.
He takes a child, places the child among them, takes the child in his arms. Visual parable. Children had no status in ancient society.
Whoever welcomes a child in my name welcomes me and the one who sent me. Receiving the insignificant is receiving God. The kingdom elevates the lowly.
John: We saw someone driving out demons in your name. We told him to stop because he was not one of us. Sectarian instinct. Boundary policing.
Jesus: Do not stop him. No one who does a miracle in my name can soon speak evil of me. Miracles in Jesus' name authenticate. Broader kingdom than the Twelve.
Whoever is not against us is for us. Inclusive principle. Contrast with 3:29: whoever is not with me is against me. Different contexts, different applications.
Anyone who gives you a cup of water in my name will not lose their reward. Even small acts of kindness count. Nothing is forgotten.

If anyone causes one of these little ones who believe to stumble, better a millstone around their neck and thrown into the sea. Severe warning. Causing spiritual harm to believers is catastrophic. Protection of the vulnerable is paramount.

If your hand causes you to stumble, cut it off. Better to enter life maimed than to go to hell with two hands, where the fire never goes out. Hyperbole, but deadly serious. Radical amputation better than damnation. Sin must be dealt with ruthlessly.
Verse missing in many manuscripts. Likely scribal repetition.
If your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off. Better to enter life crippled than be thrown into hell with two feet. Same principle: eliminate whatever causes sin.
Verse missing in many manuscripts.

If your eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out. Better to enter the kingdom with one eye than be thrown into hell with two. Hand, foot, eye: action, direction, desire. All must be brought under control.
Where the worms do not die and the fire is not quenched. Isaiah 66:24 quoted. Gehenna, the valley of Hinnom. Eternal punishment. Conscious, unending torment.
Fire = purification or judgment? Salt = preservation or flavor? Leviticus 2:13: every offering salted. Suffering purifies; judgment awaits all.

Salt is good, but if it loses saltiness, how can you make it salty? Have salt among yourselves, and be at peace with each other. Saltless salt is useless. Disciples must retain their distinctive character. Salt = preserving, flavoring, covenant loyalty. Be at peace. Stop arguing about greatness. Chapter nine: transfiguration glory, exorcism failure, passion prediction, argument about greatness, radical teaching on sin and hell. The path to glory is through suffering, service, and self-denial.
